# Questions in Livestream 99

Live Stream #99: 9000 at 99

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:41</a> | What books belong to the Old Testament, and does including or leaving out the Apocrypha have doctrinal consequences? | He accepts the Hebrew canon, argues the Apocrypha has historical and theological problems, and says later canon adoption was tied to church-history and doctrinal disputes. | He says he personally accepts the Hebrew canon and not the extended Old Testament canon, while stressing respect for Catholic and Orthodox traditions. His case is historical and textual: early Christian use of Apocryphal books does not by itself prove canonicity, some major early figures opposed their canonical status, later adoption was shaped by Reformation and counter-Reformation pressures, and books such as Judith raise historical problems that make him reluctant to treat them as inerrant Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=2432" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:32</a> | Does Second Temple adultery law explain the lack of a male adulterer in John 7:53-8:11, and is the passage authentic? | The legal background helps explain the scene, but he treats the passage itself as textually doubtful in John while possibly preserving an early Jesus tradition. | He says the Second Temple context, in which adultery was treated as a sin a woman could commit, does help explain why only the woman appears in the story. At the same time, he notes that most early manuscripts do not contain the pericope, so he does not think it was original to John, though it may still preserve an early Jesus tradition that later became incorporated there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=2661" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:21</a> | Did he watch Jeff Durbin and James White's exchange with Brandon Robertson, and what are his thoughts? | He had not seen that exchange, so he comments only generally that he dislikes tag-team debates and presuppositional apologetics. | He first thinks of a different Durbin and White debate and then clarifies that he has not seen the Brandon Robertson exchange. He therefore limits the answer to general concerns: he dislikes tag-team debates because they drift into disconnected challenges rather than sustained engagement, and he also rejects presuppositional apologetics in favor of a maximal-facts evidentialist approach. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=3024" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:24</a> | What about theistic evolution as defended by John Walton and Andrew Loke? | He says theistic evolution has problems because it tries to force modern evolutionary theory into Genesis 1. | He says theistic evolution combines problems from both sides by trying to read modern evolutionary development into the six days of Genesis 1. In his view, that shoehorns a scientific theory into an ancient Near Eastern religious text whose focus is the ordering of sacred space, and it risks the same genre and context mistakes he criticizes in other compatibilist readings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=3327" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:27</a> | Does `according to their kinds` in Genesis rule out theistic evolution? | Not necessarily. He said it mainly rules out extreme `hopeful monster` style transformation, not every evolutionary model. | He interprets the phrase as saying offspring resemble their parents, not that created kinds must remain completely static. It would rule out a random monster-style model in which one creature suddenly produces a radically different creature, but it does not by itself exclude all evolutionary or divergent-breeding models. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=3568" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:28</a> | What is the genre of the book of Jonah? | He says it uses multiple genres, including narrative, historical material, poetry, and prophetic literature. | He says Jonah should not be reduced to one genre because the book moves among narrative, historical material, poetry, and prophetic literature. Jonah 2 is poetry, and he uses that as an example of why readers must assess genre passage by passage rather than assuming a whole biblical book stays in one mode. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=4032" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:12</a> | Did Michael Heiser argue there was no rebellion of angels before the fall, and does he agree? | He broadly agrees with Heiser's sequence of three rebellions: humanity, angels, and Babel. | He summarizes Heiser's view as three great rebellions: the fall of humanity first, the angelic rebellion soon after, and then Babel. He says he has no strong disagreement with that sequence, though he may differ from Heiser on details of interpretation and thinks the seeds of rebellion may already have been present before the fall. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=4356" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:36</a> | Why were David's sons allowed to be priests in 2 Samuel 8:18? | He says `priests` there means royal or government ministers, not priests of the Lord in the temple. | He reads the verse in the context of David's government officials, not temple service. The Hebrew term can fall within a broader lexical range, so David's sons are called priests or ministers in an administrative sense, comparable to government ministers, rather than priests of the Lord performing temple duties. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=5093" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:53</a> | Could Abraham have traveled from Ur to Canaan on the Mari or Old Babylonian trade route? | Yes. He says that route crossed northern Mesopotamia and the Fertile Crescent, so Abraham could have used it. | He sees no reason Abraham could not have used that route, since it extended across northern Mesopotamia and through the Fertile Crescent. He adds that the route forked in multiple places, so travelers would have known a network of possible paths and could take the branch most appropriate for their journey. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=5168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:08</a> | What is a steel-man case against the late Exodus? | The best attack would challenge whether the group at Avaris actually left Egypt rather than merely relocating within Egypt, though he does not find it compelling. | He says a serious critic should avoid caricatures about the plagues and instead represent the biblical claim as strongly as possible. The strongest challenge would grant a community at Avaris and then ask whether the evidence shows an actual movement from Avaris to Canaan, rather than a relocation elsewhere inside Egypt; he thinks there are replies to that argument and does not find it especially strong. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=5479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:19</a> | What are his thoughts on C. John Collins's work on Genesis? | He has not read Collins's Genesis work, though he knows Collins's apocalyptic-literature scholarship. | He says he cannot really comment on Collins's Genesis work because he has not read it, though he may own the book and is aware Collins has worked in that area. He knows Collins better for apocalyptic literature, where he regards Collins's work as probably definitive, but he keeps the Genesis answer limited because he has not done that reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=5578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:58</a> | Are there pictures of aliens in hieroglyphics, and how could pyramids be built without aliens? | No. He points to Egyptian building traditions, canals, boats, ramps, pulleys, worker villages, and construction evidence. | He flatly rejects the alien claim and says pyramid building developed from Egyptian tomb-building traditions, with mastabas stacked into pyramid forms over centuries. He points to canals and ports for moving stone by boat, ramps and pulley systems for lifting blocks, evidence from quarry installations, worker villages, and remains such as broken bones and food traces as ordinary archaeological evidence for human construction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=6119" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:59</a> | Was archaic language used in antiquity to give literature a foreign flavor, and could that explain Egyptian loanwords in Exodus? | He says archaizing usually signaled prestige or classic style, not foreignness, so it does not really explain the Exodus loanwords. | He says he has not seen archaic language used to make literature sound foreign in Egyptian or biblical examples. Archaic language, such as Middle Egyptian in later documents, tends to signal refinement, education, or classical style, so Egyptian loanwords in Exodus are better explained by real Egyptian linguistic influence rather than by a literary attempt to create foreign flavor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=6414" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:54</a> | Do current seminaries teach about engaging the spirit world, magic, occult topics, or vampires? | Very rarely. He knew of one seminary course, but says schools tend to avoid such subjects despite their pastoral usefulness. | He says such courses are very rare and recalls Bruce Fields teaching a seminary course on the spirit world only after having to fight for it. The course covered angels, demons, vampires, lycanthropy, shamanism, witchcraft, magic, possession, demonic influence, and exorcism; he found it useful and argues Christians may need training even when dealing with cultures or people who only believe such things are real. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=7061" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:41</a> | What does he mean by `strange` paranormal stories from seminary? | He means reports such as electrical interference, lights flickering, flying objects, poltergeist-like activity, and seeing spirits. | He says people reported experiences such as electrical appliances or street lights flickering, light bulbs blowing out, flying objects, poltergeist-like activity, telekinesis, ghostly figures, and seeing spirits. He frames these as the kinds of accounts people brought from earlier involvement with witchcraft or similar practices and later had to think through after becoming Christians. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=7184" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:44</a> | Is Ezekiel 26 a failed prophecy against Tyre? | He argued no, reading it as a many-nations judgment in which Nebuchadnezzar is only the first major agent. | He says it is not a failed prophecy because the text itself speaks of many nations coming against Tyre. He points to cuneiform evidence that Tyre moved from being targeted for conquest to being treated as part of the Babylonian sphere, and he reads Nebuchadnezzar as one agent in a broader judgment rather than the sole fulfillment of every line. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=7765" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:25</a> | What is the earliest New Testament manuscript found outside Egypt, and why are the oldest ones from Egypt? | He says non-Egyptian finds are later, often Antioch-area and mid-third-century; Egypt preserves older manuscripts because of dry conditions. | He says the oldest New Testament manuscripts are generally from Alexandria, with examples such as Rylands Papyrus P52 dating very early, while Antioch-area material is typically later, around the mid-third century. He attributes the difference mostly to preservation: Egypt's dry conditions protect papyrus far better than wetter environments, so older material is more likely to survive there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=7995" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:15</a> | Is there scholarly substance behind an early date for Revelation before Jerusalem's fall in AD 70? | Not much, in his view. He favors a late first-century date and finds preterist arguments unpersuasive. | He says he does not see much evidence for a pre-70 date and regards that dating as especially important to preterist readings. He leans toward early church historians who place Revelation in the later first century, around the Domitianic period, and says appeals to Jerusalem's fall or tribulation language are not enough because many periods could be described as severe tribulation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=8345" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:05</a> | What about sin offerings during the millennium in Ezekiel 40-48? | He gives only a general answer: sacrifices continue, sin offerings did not themselves forgive sin, and a memorial or thanksgiving role is possible. | He keeps the answer deliberately broad because Ezekiel 40-48 is too much material for a quick response. He says temple ritual and offerings continue in the millennial period, but sin offerings are tricky because biblical sin offerings are tied to ritual categories such as Yom Kippur, trespass, and iniquity and do not themselves forgive sin; he suggests a memorial or thanksgiving function may be possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=8894" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:14</a> | How does he reconcile his view on Urkesh with Acts 7:4? | He says `land of the Chaldeans` may reflect a textual corruption or later development that became historically apt. | He says Acts 7:4 is using the best text available at the time, with the language of the Chaldeans already present in the textual tradition. On his view, an original reference to Urkesh was corrupted into a form associated with Chaldeans, and because Chaldeans later moved into that region, the wording became historically apt even if it began as a textual development or anachronistic label. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=9437" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:17</a> | Do Genesis 1 parallels in other cultures imply oral descent from Adam, and is that only realistic with young-earth creationism? | Oral descent is possible, but he says it is not limited to young-earth creationism and he avoids firm compatibilist models. | He says similar creation-story elements could imply oral descent, and he has no problem with that possibility. However, he rejects the claim that this only works with young-earth creationism because other compatibilist models could also account for it, and he avoids endorsing any firm harmonizing model because he thinks the evidence is not clear enough. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=9600" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:00</a> | What does he think about the Discovery Channel and History Channel, especially as sources for aliens-and-hieroglyphics claims? | He says they are entertainment, not reliable history, and specifically rejects `ancient aliens` style readings of hieroglyphs. | He says the History Channel is largely not about history and that Discovery and similar cable channels have shifted toward entertainment as the cable industry changed. He therefore does not treat them as reliable sources for historical claims, especially claims about aliens in hieroglyphics or other ancient-aliens style programming. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=9997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:37</a> | What is his opinion on Gobekli Tepe and its relevance for a model of history? | It is a very old ritual site tied to early agriculture in Anatolia; he says excavation is ongoing and historically important. | He describes Gobekli Tepe as an extremely old ritual site, perhaps 10,000 to 12,000 years old, in Anatolia. Its relevance is that it appears near the development and domestication of grains such as emmer wheat, barley, and chickpeas, has carved stone pillars and large ritual architecture, and remains an ongoing excavation that may reveal more through surrounding settlements. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=10997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:17</a> | Is Anubis a bad god, and did he have a brother named Beerus? | No Beerus. Anubis was neither simply good nor bad, and Egyptian gods could have both benevolent and dangerous aspects. | He says Beerus is not part of Anubis's family and then explains that Egyptian gods were not simply good or bad. Anubis could protect the dead on their journey through the Duat, but if hostile could abandon them to its dangers; his half-brother in Egyptian mythology would be Horus, since both are connected to Osiris but have different mothers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=11307" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:27</a> | Did Old Testament offerings have any Egyptian parallels? | Some shared reciprocal-economy logic exists, but Egyptian offerings mainly fed gods and the dead while Israelite offerings had different ritual categories and purposes. | He says the systems are different because Israelite offerings are highly differentiated, including sin, oath, thanksgiving, and atonement-related offerings, while Egyptian offerings mainly fed gods or the deceased. The main parallel is reciprocal economy: giving an offering in expectation of divine return or blessing, but he stresses that Egypt did not have the same specific categories as Israel's sin or oath offerings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=11820" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:00</a> | If overindulgence is not gluttony, is it still a sin? | Not necessarily. He defines biblical gluttony as taking more than one's share so others suffer, while noting overindulgence can still become sinful in some circumstances. | He says overindulgence is not automatically sinful because biblical feast days themselves involved abundant eating and drinking. The biblical problem of gluttony is taking more than one's share in a subsistence society, where doing so could leave someone else hungry or dead; overindulgence can still become sinful in some contexts, but it is not identical to gluttony. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=12328" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:28</a> | Who is his favorite Christian philosopher historically, and why? | Soren Kierkegaard, especially for devotional Christian reflection rather than existentialism generally. | He names Soren Kierkegaard, though he says he is not especially fond of existentialist philosophy in general. What he appreciates is Kierkegaard's devout Christian reflection, especially a work he recalls as dealing with the problem of being a Christian and the difficulty of reconciling Christian life with the surrounding world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/X1Q7ZZ5Q6vg?t=12546" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:06</a> | Should historians be able to investigate miracles as historians, and why or why not? | Historians can study claims, witnesses, and surrounding evidence, but proving the miracle itself usually exceeds historical method. | He says historians can ask historical questions around miracles, such as whether people claimed to see one, whether witnesses were credible, and whether the claim appears in contemporary sources. But the miracle itself normally exceeds historical method because history depends on corroborating evidence, and proving the physical reality of an event such as walking on water or resurrection would require more than testimony alone. |
